title: Find floor and ceil in sorted array
description: Find the floor and ceil values of a given element in a sorted array

examples.explanation:
Initialize floor = None, ceil = None
Iterate arr:
  i=0: num=1 ≤5 → floor=1 (since floor=None); num=1 ≥5? No
  i=1: num=2 ≤5 → floor=2 (since 2>1); num=2 ≥5? No
  i=2: num=8 ≤5? No; num=8 ≥5 → ceil=8 (since ceil=None)
  i=3: num=10 ≤5? No; num=10 ≥5 → ceil stays 8 (since 10>8)
  i=4: num=10 ≤5? No; num=10 ≥5 → ceil stays 8
  i=5: num=12 ≤5? No; num=12 ≥5 → ceil stays 8
  i=6: num=19 ≤5? No; num=19 ≥5 → ceil stays 8
Return floor=2, ceil=8

Initialize floor = None, ceil = None
Iterate arr:
  i=0: num=5 ≤12 → floor=5; num=5 ≥12? No
  i=1: num=10 ≤12 → floor=10 (since 10>5); num=10 ≥12? No
  i=2: num=15 ≤12? No; num=15 ≥12 → ceil=15
Return floor=10, ceil=15

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n)
approaches.summary: Iterate through the sorted array linearly to find the floor (largest element ≤ target) and ceil (smallest element ≥ target). Use simple comparisons to track these values as you scan each element.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find Floor and Ceil in Sorted Array Using Linear Scan
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of finding floor and ceil values for a target in a sorted array using brute force O(n) approach.

examples.trace:
Initialize floor = None, ceil = None
Iterate arr:
  i=0: num=1 ≤5 → floor=1 (since floor=None); num=1 ≥5? No
  i=1: num=2 ≤5 → floor=2 (since 2>1); num=2 ≥5? No
  i=2: num=8 ≤5? No; num=8 ≥5 → ceil=8 (since ceil=None)
  i=3: num=10 ≤5? No; num=10 ≥5 → ceil stays 8 (since 10>8)
  i=4: num=10 ≤5? No; num=10 ≥5 → ceil stays 8
  i=5: num=12 ≤5? No; num=12 ≥5 → ceil stays 8
  i=6: num=19 ≤5? No; num=19 ≥5 → ceil stays 8
Return floor=2, ceil=8

Initialize floor = None, ceil = None
Iterate arr:
  i=0: num=5 ≤12 → floor=5; num=5 ≥12? No
  i=1: num=10 ≤12 → floor=10 (since 10>5); num=10 ≥12? No

Return floor=10, ceil=15

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Compare and Update Floor/Ceil for a Single Element
ladders.desc: The core comparison logic that updates floor and ceil when examining one array element against target.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the brute force approach. For each element in the array, we need to determine if it should become the new floor or ceil. The intuition is based on the definitions: floor is the largest element ≤ target, so we update floor only when the current element is ≤ target AND greater than the current floor. Similarly, ceil is the smallest element ≥ target, so we update ceil only when the current element is ≥ target AND smaller than the current ceil. We use two independent if conditions (not else-if) because an element could be both floor and ceil if it equals the target. The time complexity for this sub-routine is O(1) per element, and space is O(1). Common mistakes include using else-if which would miss updating both when element equals target, or incorrect comparison operators (e.g., using < instead of ≤). This feeds into L0 by being called in a loop for each array element. Real code pattern: if num <= target: update floor; if num >= target: update ceil.

examples.trace:
Check num=5 vs target=5:
  Condition1: 5 ≤ 5 → True, and 5 > current_floor=2 → True, so floor=5
  Condition2: 5 ≥ 5 → True, and 5 < current_ceil=8 → True, so ceil=5

Check num=12 vs target=12:
  Condition1: 12 ≤ 12 → True, and 12 > current_floor=10 → True, so floor=12
  Condition2: 12 ≥ 12 → True, and 12 < current_ceil=15 → True, so ceil=12

ladders.title: Core Logic — Maintaining Best Candidates During Linear Traversal
ladders.desc: The technique of iterating through a sorted array while tracking the best floor and ceil values seen so far.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the core algorithmic technique of maintaining running maximum (for floor) and minimum (for ceil) candidates during a linear scan. The intuition leverages the sorted property: as we move left to right, elements increase, so when we find a new floor candidate, it will be larger than previous ones if it's ≤ target. Similarly, once we find a ceil candidate, later elements will be larger, so we only update ceil if we find a smaller one that's still ≥ target. We initialize floor to -∞ (or sentinel) and ceil to +∞ to represent 'not found'. The algorithm scans each element, applying the comparison logic from L1. Time O(n), space O(1). Common mistakes include not initializing properly or stopping early incorrectly. This feeds into L1 by providing the loop structure. Real code pattern: initialize; for num in arr: update floor/ceil.

examples.trace:
Initialize floor=None, ceil=None
Step1: num=1 → floor=1, ceil unchanged
Step2: num=2 → floor=2 (since 2>1), ceil unchanged
Step3: num=8 → floor unchanged, ceil=8
Step4: num=10 → floor unchanged, ceil unchanged (10>8)
Final: floor=2, ceil=8

Initialize floor=None, ceil=None
Step1: num=3 → floor=3, ceil unchanged
Step2: num=7 → floor unchanged, ceil=7
Step3: num=9 → floor unchanged, ceil unchanged
Final: floor=3, ceil=7

ladders.title: Building Block — Comparing Numbers with Inequality Conditions
ladders.desc: Understanding how to use ≤ and ≥ comparisons to determine if a number is floor or ceil candidate.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves comparing a number against a target using inequality operators to decide its role. For floor: we need numbers that are less than or equal to target (≤). For ceil: we need numbers greater than or equal to target (≥). The intuition is that floor represents values not exceeding target, while ceil represents values not below target. We use inclusive comparisons (≤ and ≥) because if a number equals target, it can serve as both floor and ceil. This is a fundamental building block for the update logic in L2. Time and space are O(1). Common mistakes include using strict inequalities (< or >) which would exclude equality cases. This feeds into L2 by providing the condition checks. Real code pattern: if x <= target: ...; if x >= target: ...

examples.trace:
Check floor: 5 ≤ 5 → True, so is_floor_candidate=True
Check ceil: 5 ≥ 5 → True, so is_ceil_candidate=True

Check floor: 3 ≤ 5 → True, so is_floor_candidate=True
Check ceil: 3 ≥ 5 → False, so is_ceil_candidate=False

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterating Through an Array with a Loop
ladders.desc: Fundamental operation of traversing each element in an array sequentially using a loop.
ladders.explanation: This is a basic programming operation: using a loop (e.g., for or while) to access each element in an array one by one. The intuition is that to examine all elements, we need to visit each index from start to end. For an array of size n, we iterate from i=0 to n-1. This operation has O(n) time and O(1) space. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors (e.g., going beyond array bounds) or using wrong loop types. This feeds into L3 by providing the mechanism to apply comparisons to each element. Real code pattern: for i in range(len(arr)): x = arr[i]

examples.trace:
Initialize i=0
Step1: i=0 → access arr[0]=1
Step2: i=1 → access arr[1]=2
Step3: i=2 → access arr[2]=3
End loop as i reaches length 3

Initialize i=0
Step1: i=0 → access arr[0]=5
Step2: i=1 → access arr[1]=10
End loop

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Floor and Ceil Definitions
ladders.desc: The simplest prerequisite: what floor and ceil mean for a target value in a set of numbers.
ladders.explanation: This foundational concept defines floor and ceil without any code. Floor of a target is the largest number in a set that is less than or equal to the target. Ceil is the smallest number in the set that is greater than or equal to the target. Intuition: imagine numbers on a number line; floor is the closest number from left (≤ target), ceil is from right (≥ target). If target exists in set, both floor and ceil equal target. If no number ≤ target, floor is undefined (None). If no number ≥ target, ceil is undefined. This is pure mathematical understanding. Time/space not applicable. Common mistakes include confusing floor with 'less than' (strict) or ceil with 'greater than'. This feeds into L4 by providing the goal for the algorithm. Real code pattern: conceptual only.

examples.trace:
Numbers ≤5: 1,2 → largest is 2 → floor=2
Numbers ≥5: 8,10 → smallest is 8 → ceil=8

Numbers ≤10: 5,10 → largest is 10 → floor=10
Numbers ≥10: 10,15 → smallest is 10 → ceil=10

approaches.name: Binary Search O(log n)
approaches.summary: Use binary search to efficiently locate the target or its neighbors in the sorted array. Adjust the search to find the floor and ceil by handling cases where the target is present, absent, or at boundaries.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find Floor and Ceil in Sorted Array Using Binary Search
ladders.desc: Complete solution to find floor and ceil of a target in sorted array using binary search.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires finding both floor (largest element ≤ target) and ceil (smallest element ≥ target) of a given target in a sorted array. The binary search approach efficiently handles this in O(log n) time by maintaining two pointers (low and high) and adjusting them based on comparisons with the target. The intuition is that in a sorted array, binary search can locate the target's position or its neighbors by narrowing the search space. The algorithm initializes floor and ceil to sentinel values (e.g., -1 for floor, -1 for ceil). During binary search, if arr[mid] equals target, both floor and ceil are set to arr[mid]. If arr[mid] < target, update floor to arr[mid] and move low to mid+1 to search right for a larger floor or ceil. If arr[mid] > target, update ceil to arr[mid] and move high to mid-1 to search left for a smaller ceil or floor. After the loop, return floor and ceil. Time complexity is O(log n) due to halving search space, space is O(1) using only pointers. This connects to L1 by implementing the full binary search logic. Common mistakes include not handling empty arrays, off-by-one errors in pointer updates, and incorrect initialization of floor/ceil. Real code pattern: def findFloorCeil(arr, target): floor, ceil = -1, -1; low, high = 0, len(arr)-1; while low <= high: mid = (low+high)//2; if arr[mid] == target: return arr[mid], arr[mid]; elif arr[mid] < target: floor = arr[mid]; low = mid+1; else: ceil = arr[mid]; high = mid-1; return floor, ceil.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize floor=-1, ceil=-1, low=0, high=6. Step 2: mid=(0+6)//2=3, arr[3]=10 > 5, so ceil=10, high=2. Step 3: low=0, high=2, mid=(0+2)//2=1, arr[1]=2 < 5, so floor=2, low=2. Step 4: low=2, high=2, mid=(2+2)//2=2, arr[2]=8 > 5, so ceil=8, high=1. Step 5: low=2 > high=1, exit loop. Return floor=2, ceil=8.
Step 1: Initialize floor=-1, ceil=-1, low=0, high=3. Step 2: mid=(0+3)//2=1, arr[1]=3 < 5, so floor=3, low=2. Step 3: low=2, high=3, mid=(2+3)//2=2, arr[2]=5 == 5, so return floor=5, ceil=5.

ladders.title: Key Sub-Routine — Binary Search for Floor and Ceil Updates
ladders.desc: Implement binary search loop that updates floor and ceil pointers based on comparisons.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine focuses on the core binary search loop that iteratively updates floor and ceil values. The intuition is that by comparing the middle element with the target, we can decide which half of the array to search next while tracking potential floor and ceil candidates. The algorithm uses low and high pointers to define the search range. At each step, compute mid = (low+high)//2. If arr[mid] equals target, both floor and ceil are set to arr[mid] and search terminates. If arr[mid] < target, arr[mid] is a candidate for floor (since it's ≤ target), so update floor = arr[mid] and move low = mid+1 to search the right half for a larger floor or ceil. If arr[mid] > target, arr[mid] is a candidate for ceil (since it's ≥ target), so update ceil = arr[mid] and move high = mid-1 to search the left half for a smaller ceil or floor. The loop continues until low > high. Time complexity is O(log n) as the search space halves each iteration, space is O(1). This feeds into L0 by providing the iterative logic for the full solution. Common mistakes include incorrect updates (e.g., updating floor when arr[mid] > target) or infinite loops due to pointer errors. Real code pattern: while low <= high: mid = (low+high)//2; if arr[mid] == target: floor = ceil = arr[mid]; break; elif arr[mid] < target: floor = arr[mid]; low = mid+1; else: ceil = arr[mid]; high = mid-1.

examples.input: arr = [1, 2, 8, 10, 12, 19], target = 5, initial floor=-1, ceil=-1, low=0, high=5
examples.trace: Step 1: mid=(0+5)//2=2, arr[2]=8 > 5, so ceil=8, high=1. Step 2: low=0, high=1, mid=(0+1)//2=0, arr[0]=1 < 5, so floor=1, low=1. Step 3: low=1, high=1, mid=(1+1)//2=1, arr[1]=2 < 5, so floor=2, low=2. Step 4: low=2 > high=1, exit. Final floor=2, ceil=8.

examples.input: arr = [3, 7, 9], target = 7, initial floor=-1, ceil=-1, low=0, high=2
examples.trace: Step 1: mid=(0+2)//2=1, arr[1]=7 == 7, so floor=7, ceil=7, break. Exit loop.

testCases.input: arr = [1, 2, 8, 10, 12, 19], target = 5, low=0, high=5, floor=-1, ceil=-1
arr = [3, 7, 9], target = 7, low=0, high=2, floor=-1, ceil=-1
arr = [1, 3, 5], target = 0, low=0, high=2, floor=-1, ceil=-1
arr = [4, 6, 8], target = 10, low=0, high=2, floor=-1, ceil=-1
arr = [2, 4, 6, 8], target = 5, low=0, high=3, floor=-1, ceil=-1
arr = [10], target = 10, low=0, high=0, floor=-1, ceil=-1
arr = [1, 5], target = 3, low=0, high=1, floor=-1, ceil=-1
arr = [6, 9, 12], target = 9, low=0, high=2, floor=-1, ceil=-1
arr = [3, 6, 9, 12], target = 7, low=0, high=3, floor=-1, ceil=-1
arr = [], target = 5, low=0, high=-1, floor=-1, ceil=-1

ladders.title: Core Logic — Comparing Middle Element to Update Floor and Ceil
ladders.desc: Determine how to update floor and ceil based on comparison of arr[mid] with target.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the decision logic at each step of binary search: comparing the middle element (arr[mid]) with the target to update floor and ceil. The intuition is that in a sorted array, if arr[mid] equals target, it serves as both floor and ceil. If arr[mid] is less than target, it is a candidate for floor (since floor is the largest element ≤ target), so we update floor to arr[mid] and search right for potentially larger values. If arr[mid] is greater than target, it is a candidate for ceil (since ceil is the smallest element ≥ target), so we update ceil to arr[mid] and search left for potentially smaller values. This logic ensures that floor and ceil are always the best candidates found so far. The algorithm involves a simple if-elif-else structure: if arr[mid] == target: set both; elif arr[mid] < target: update floor; else: update ceil. Time complexity per comparison is O(1), space is O(1). This connects to L1 by being the conditional check within the binary search loop. Common mistakes include swapping the conditions or not handling equality correctly. Real code pattern: if arr[mid] == target: floor = ceil = arr[mid]; elif arr[mid] < target: floor = arr[mid]; else: ceil = arr[mid].

examples.trace: Step 1: Compare 8 and 5. Since 8 > 5, update ceil to 8 (as 8 is ≥ 5). Floor remains -1. No further action in this step.
Step 1: Compare 3 and 5. Since 3 < 5, update floor to 3 (as 3 is ≤ 5 and larger than previous floor 2). Ceil remains 10.

ladders.title: Building Block — Binary Search Pointer Updates
ladders.desc: Update low and high pointers in binary search based on comparison result.
ladders.explanation: This level covers how to adjust the search range (low and high pointers) in binary search after comparing arr[mid] with the target. The intuition is that the sorted array property allows us to eliminate half of the search space each step. If arr[mid] is less than target, the target (or its floor/ceil) must be in the right half (indices mid+1 to high), so set low = mid+1. If arr[mid] is greater than target, the target (or its floor/ceil) must be in the left half (indices low to mid-1), so set high = mid-1. If arr[mid] equals target, the search can terminate, so pointers are not updated further. This ensures the algorithm converges in O(log n) time. The algorithm: after comparing, if arr[mid] < target: low = mid+1; if arr[mid] > target: high = mid-1. Time complexity per update is O(1), space is O(1). This feeds into L2 by providing the mechanism to narrow the search. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors (e.g., setting low = mid instead of mid+1) or not handling the equality case. Real code pattern: if arr[mid] < target: low = mid+1; elif arr[mid] > target: high = mid-1.

examples.trace: Step 1: Compare arr[mid]=8 with target=5. Since 8 > 5, update high = mid-1 = 2-1 = 1. Low remains 0.
Step 1: Compare arr[mid]=7 with target=7. Since equal, no pointer updates needed; search stops.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Compute Middle Index in Binary Search
ladders.desc: Calculate the middle index (mid) given low and high pointers.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on computing the middle index in binary search, a fundamental operation to split the search range. The intuition is that to efficiently search a sorted array, we need to examine the middle element to decide which half to proceed with. The formula is mid = (low + high) // 2, using integer division to get the floor value. This ensures mid is an integer index within the range [low, high]. For example, if low=0 and high=5, mid=(0+5)//2=2. In programming, this prevents overflow in some languages by using low + (high-low)//2, but here we use the simple version. The algorithm: given low and high, compute mid = (low + high) // 2. Time complexity is O(1), space is O(1). This connects to L3 by providing the index needed for comparisons and pointer updates. Common mistakes include using floating-point division or incorrect rounding. Real code pattern: mid = (low + high) // 2.

examples.trace: Step 1: Compute mid = (0 + 6) // 2 = 6 // 2 = 3.
Step 1: Compute mid = (2 + 5) // 2 = 7 // 2 = 3.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Floor and Ceil in Sorted Arrays
ladders.desc: Define floor and ceil concepts for a target in a sorted array.
ladders.explanation: This foundational level introduces the concepts of floor and ceil for a given target in a sorted array, essential for beginners. Floor is defined as the largest element in the array that is less than or equal to the target. If no such element exists (e.g., target is smaller than all elements), floor is -1 or another sentinel value. Ceil is defined as the smallest element in the array that is greater than or equal to the target. If no such element exists (e.g., target is larger than all elements), ceil is -1. The intuition is that in a sorted array, these values represent the nearest neighbors of the target. For example, in array [1, 3, 5, 7], for target=4, floor=3 (largest ≤4) and ceil=5 (smallest ≥4). The algorithm involves scanning the array, but here we just define the concepts. Time complexity for a full scan would be O(n), but this level is conceptual. This feeds into L4 by establishing what we aim to compute. Common mistakes include confusing floor with ceil or not handling edge cases. Real code pattern: Concept only, no code; e.g., floor = max(arr[i] where arr[i] ≤ target), ceil = min(arr[i] where arr[i] ≥ target).

examples.trace: Step 1: Identify elements ≤5: [1, 2]. Largest is 2, so floor=2. Step 2: Identify elements ≥5: [8, 10, 12, 19]. Smallest is 8, so ceil=8.
Step 1: Identify elements ≤7: [3, 7]. Largest is 7, so floor=7. Step 2: Identify elements ≥7: [7, 9]. Smallest is 7, so ceil=7.

approaches.name: Two Separate Binary Searches O(log n)
approaches.summary: Perform two independent binary searches: one to find the floor and another to find the ceil. This approach can simplify logic by treating each search as a distinct problem, though it may involve redundant computations.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find Floor and Ceil with Two Binary Searches
ladders.desc: Complete solution using two independent binary searches to find floor and ceil values in a sorted array.
ladders.explanation: This approach solves the problem by performing two separate binary searches: one to find the floor (largest element ≤ target) and another to find the ceil (smallest element ≥ target). The intuition is that each search can be treated as a distinct problem with clear termination conditions, simplifying the logic compared to a single combined search. For floor search, we maintain a candidate floor value and update it whenever we find an element ≤ target, moving left to find larger candidates or right to continue searching. For ceil search, we maintain a candidate ceil value and update it when finding an element ≥ target, moving right to find smaller candidates or left to continue. The algorithm: 1) Initialize floor = -1, ceil = -1. 2) Perform binary search for floor: set low=0, high=n-1; while low≤high: mid=(low+high)//2; if arr[mid]≤target: update floor=arr[mid], low=mid+1 to search right for larger floor; else: high=mid-1 to search left. 3) Perform binary search for ceil: low=0, high=n-1; while low≤high: mid=(low+high)//2; if arr[mid]≥target: update ceil=arr[mid], high=mid-1 to search left for smaller ceil; else: low=mid+1 to search right. 4) Return (floor, ceil). Time complexity is O(log n) for each search, total O(log n). Space is O(1). Connection to L1: This uses the key sub-routine of binary search twice. Common mistakes: not handling empty arrays (return (-1,-1)), confusing update conditions, or infinite loops from incorrect bounds updates. Real code pattern: def find_floor_ceil(arr, target): floor, ceil = -1, -1; low, high = 0, len(arr)-1; while low <= high: mid = (low+high)//2; if arr[mid] <= target: floor = arr[mid]; low = mid+1; else: high = mid-1; low, high = 0, len(arr)-1; while low <= high: mid = (low+high)//2; if arr[mid] >= target: ceil = arr[mid]; high = mid-1; else: low = mid+1; return (floor, ceil).

examples.trace: Floor search: low=0, high=6, mid=3, arr[3]=10>5 → high=2; low=0, high=2, mid=1, arr[1]=2≤5 → floor=2, low=2; low=2, high=2, mid=2, arr[2]=8>5 → high=1; low=2>high, stop. Ceil search: low=0, high=6, mid=3, arr[3]=10≥5 → ceil=10, high=2; low=0, high=2, mid=1, arr[1]=2<5 → low=2; low=2, high=2, mid=2, arr[2]=8≥5 → ceil=8, high=1; low=2>high, stop. Return (2,8).

Floor search: low=0, high=2, mid=1, arr[1]=10≤12 → floor=10, low=2; low=2, high=2, mid=2, arr[2]=15>12 → high=1; low=2>high, stop. Ceil search: low=0, high=2, mid=1, arr[1]=10<12 → low=2; low=2, high=2, mid=2, arr[2]=15≥12 → ceil=15, high=1; low=2>high, stop. Return (10,15).

ladders.title: Key Sub-Routine — Binary Search for Floor in Sorted Array
ladders.desc: Find the floor (largest element ≤ target) in a sorted array using binary search.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine finds the floor of a target in a sorted array, which is the largest element that is less than or equal to the target. The intuition leverages binary search's efficiency by repeatedly halving the search space. We maintain a candidate floor value initialized to -1 (indicating no floor found). At each step, we check the middle element: if it is ≤ target, it's a potential floor—we update the candidate and move to the right half to search for a larger floor; if it is > target, we move to the left half to find smaller elements. The algorithm: 1) Initialize floor = -1, low = 0, high = n-1. 2) While low ≤ high: mid = (low + high) // 2; if arr[mid] ≤ target: floor = arr[mid], low = mid + 1; else: high = mid - 1. 3) Return floor. Time complexity is O(log n), space O(1). Connection to L0: This is one of the two searches used in the full problem. Common mistakes: forgetting to update floor when arr[mid] == target, or incorrect bounds updates leading to missed elements. Real code pattern: def find_floor(arr, target): floor = -1; low, high = 0, len(arr)-1; while low <= high: mid = (low+high)//2; if arr[mid] <= target: floor = arr[mid]; low = mid+1; else: high = mid-1; return floor.

examples.trace: low=0, high=6, mid=3, arr[3]=10>5 → high=2; low=0, high=2, mid=1, arr[1]=2≤5 → floor=2, low=2; low=2, high=2, mid=2, arr[2]=8>5 → high=1; low=2>high, stop. Return 2.
low=0, high=2, mid=1, arr[1]=10≤12 → floor=10, low=2; low=2, high=2, mid=2, arr[2]=15>12 → high=1; low=2>high, stop. Return 10.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Binary Search with Conditional Updates
ladders.desc: Binary search that updates a result based on a condition (e.g., ≤ or ≥ target).
ladders.explanation: This core logic extends basic binary search by incorporating conditional updates to find a specific element meeting criteria, such as floor or ceil. The intuition is that binary search efficiently narrows the search space, and we can track a candidate result that satisfies our condition, updating it as we find better candidates. For example, to find floor, we update the result when arr[mid] ≤ target and search right for potentially larger values; to find ceil, update when arr[mid] ≥ target and search left for potentially smaller values. The algorithm: 1) Initialize result = -1, low = 0, high = n-1. 2) While low ≤ high: mid = (low + high) // 2; if condition(arr[mid], target) is true: update result, adjust bounds to search for better candidate; else: adjust bounds to continue search. 3) Return result. Time O(log n), space O(1). Connection to L1: This logic is applied in the floor and ceil searches. Common mistakes: incorrect condition logic, not updating bounds properly, or infinite loops. Real code pattern: def binary_search_conditional(arr, target, condition): result = -1; low, high = 0, len(arr)-1; while low <= high: mid = (low+high)//2; if condition(arr[mid], target): result = arr[mid]; # adjust bounds based on goal; else: # adjust bounds opposite; return result.

examples.input: arr = [1, 2, 8, 10, 10, 12, 19], target = 5, condition = lambda x, t: x <= t
examples.trace: low=0, high=6, mid=3, arr[3]=10, condition false → high=2; low=0, high=2, mid=1, arr[1]=2, condition true → result=2, low=2; low=2, high=2, mid=2, arr[2]=8, condition false → high=1; low=2>high, stop. Return 2.

examples.input: arr = [5, 10, 15], target = 12, condition = lambda x, t: x >= t
examples.trace: low=0, high=2, mid=1, arr[1]=10, condition false → low=2; low=2, high=2, mid=2, arr[2]=15, condition true → result=15, high=1; low=2>high, stop. Return 15.

testCases.input: arr = [1, 2, 8, 10, 10, 12, 19], target = 5, condition = lambda x, t: x <= t
arr = [1, 2, 8, 10, 10, 12, 19], target = 5, condition = lambda x, t: x >= t
arr = [5, 10, 15], target = 12, condition = lambda x, t: x <= t
arr = [5, 10, 15], target = 12, condition = lambda x, t: x >= t
arr = [3], target = 3, condition = lambda x, t: x <= t
arr = [3], target = 3, condition = lambda x, t: x >= t
arr = [], target = 5, condition = lambda x, t: x <= t
arr = [1, 3, 5], target = 4, condition = lambda x, t: x <= t
arr = [1, 3, 5], target = 4, condition = lambda x, t: x >= t
arr = [2, 4, 6], target = 1, condition = lambda x, t: x >= t

ladders.title: Building Block — Binary Search in Sorted Array
ladders.desc: Standard binary search to find if an element exists in a sorted array.
ladders.explanation: Binary search is an efficient algorithm for finding an element in a sorted array by repeatedly dividing the search interval in half. The intuition is that because the array is sorted, we can compare the target with the middle element and eliminate half of the remaining elements based on whether the target is less than or greater than the middle element. This reduces the time complexity from O(n) for linear search to O(log n). The algorithm: 1) Initialize low = 0, high = n-1. 2) While low ≤ high: mid = (low + high) // 2; if arr[mid] == target: return mid (or True); else if arr[mid] < target: low = mid + 1; else: high = mid - 1. 3) Return -1 (or False) if not found. Time O(log n), space O(1). Connection to L2: This is the foundation for conditional binary searches. Common mistakes: off-by-one errors in bounds, incorrect termination condition, or integer overflow in mid calculation. Real code pattern: def binary_search(arr, target): low, high = 0, len(arr)-1; while low <= high: mid = (low+high)//2; if arr[mid] == target: return mid; elif arr[mid] < target: low = mid+1; else: high = mid-1; return -1.

examples.trace: low=0, high=6, mid=3, arr[3]=10 == target → return 3.
low=0, high=2, mid=1, arr[1]=10 < 12 → low=2; low=2, high=2, mid=2, arr[2]=15 > 12 → high=1; low=2>high, stop. Return -1.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterating with While Loop and Mid Calculation
ladders.desc: Using a while loop to iterate and calculate mid index in an array.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves using a while loop to repeatedly execute code until a condition is met, combined with calculating the middle index of a range. In algorithms like binary search, we use a while loop to continue searching as long as the low index is less than or equal to the high index. The mid index is calculated as (low + high) // 2 using integer division to find the midpoint. The intuition is that loops allow repetitive actions, and mid calculation helps divide the search space. The algorithm: 1) Initialize low and high. 2) While low ≤ high: mid = (low + high) // 2; perform some action; update low or high based on conditions. 3) Exit loop when condition fails. Time depends on iterations, space O(1). Connection to L3: This is how binary search implements its repetition. Common mistakes: infinite loops from not updating bounds, incorrect mid calculation leading to index errors, or using floating-point division. Real code pattern: low, high = 0, n-1; while low <= high: mid = (low + high) // 2; # do something; if condition: low = mid + 1; else: high = mid - 1.

examples.trace: Iteration 1: low=0, high=6, mid=3; update high=2. Iteration 2: low=0, high=2, mid=1; update low=2. Iteration 3: low=2, high=2, mid=2; update high=1. Iteration 4: low=2, high=1, condition false, stop.
Iteration 1: low=0, high=2, mid=1; update low=2. Iteration 2: low=2, high=2, mid=2; update high=1. Iteration 3: low=2, high=1, condition false, stop.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Array Indexing and Comparisons
ladders.desc: Accessing elements in an array and comparing values.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept involves basic operations on arrays: indexing to access elements and comparisons to evaluate their values. An array is a data structure that stores elements in contiguous memory locations, accessible via indices starting from 0. Indexing allows retrieval of an element at a specific position, e.g., arr[i] gives the element at index i. Comparisons involve operators like ==, <, >, <=, >= to check relationships between values. The intuition is that algorithms rely on accessing data and making decisions based on comparisons. For example, in binary search, we access arr[mid] and compare it with target to decide the next step. The algorithm: 1) Define an array and target. 2) Access an element using an index. 3) Compare the element with target using a comparison operator. 4) Based on result, take action. Time O(1) per access/comparison, space O(1). Connection to L4: This is used in the while loop to check conditions. Common mistakes: index out of bounds, incorrect comparison logic, or misunderstanding zero-based indexing. Real code pattern: arr = [1, 2, 3]; idx = 1; element = arr[idx]; # element is 2; if element < target: # do something.

examples.trace: Step 1: Access element at index 1: arr[1] = 10. Step 2: Compare 10 with target 12 using <: 10 < 12 evaluates to True. Step 3: Based on True, we might move to a different index.
Step 1: Access element at index 3: arr[3] = 10. Step 2: Compare 10 with target 10 using ==: 10 == 10 evaluates to True. Step 3: Based on True, we might return the index.
